Since the lake starts with 100 tons of fish and the maximum population is 100 tons, if all fishermen catch too many fish this month, the fish population will decrease significantly, reducing future income. To maximize long-term income, it is best to fish sustainably.

If each fisherman catches 10 tons, total catch is 50 tons, leaving 50 tons in the lake. After reproduction, the fish population doubles to 100 tons again, maintaining the maximum population.

Catching 10 tons this month yields $10,000, and the fish population remains stable for future months.

Answer: 10

Since the lake starts with the maximum population of 100 tons, if all fishermen catch a moderate amount, the fish population can sustain itself and continue to reproduce. If I catch too many fish this month, the population will decrease significantly, reducing future income. If I catch too few, I might miss out on immediate earnings.

To balance short-term gain and long-term sustainability, I will catch a moderate amount, say 10 tons. This way, the total catch by all fishermen might be around 50 tons (assuming others catch similar amounts), leaving 50 tons in the lake, which will double to 100 tons again next month.

Answer: 10

Therefore, I will catch 10 tons this month to balance immediate income and future sustainability.

Since the lake starts with the maximum population of 100 tons, if everyone fishes aggressively and catches a lot, the fish population will decrease and might not recover fully. To ensure a sustainable income over the long run, it is best to fish a moderate amount so that the fish population remains healthy and can reproduce to the maximum capacity.

If I catch too much this month, the fish population will drop significantly, reducing future catches. If I catch too little, I miss out on potential income this month.

Assuming others might fish moderately as well, I will choose to catch 20 tons this month. This amount balances immediate income and sustainability, encouraging others to do the same for long-term benefit.
